title: Egyptian Ful Medames

ingredients.name: dried fava beans (broad beans), soaked overnight; or use canned
visual_checks: Fava beans (broad beans) are traditional; lobia (black-eyed peas) is the closest widely available substitute
ingredients.notes: Fava beans (broad beans) are traditional; lobia (black-eyed peas) is the closest widely available substitute

subs.name: Boiled chickpeas give a neutral, creamy result; soaked and boiled toor dal is an ultra-budget substitute
Half the amount of cumin seeds, dry-roasted and crushed, for a more intense earthy note
Half a lime or 1 tsp tamarind water for a rounder sourness
Any neutral oil; a few drops of sesame oil add a distinctive aroma

steps.text: If using dried soaked fava beans, drain and transfer to a saucepan with 600 ml fresh water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer, cover and cook for 25–30 minutes until the beans are completely tender and break easily between your fingers. If using canned lobia or canned fava beans, drain, rinse and place in a saucepan with 100 ml water over low heat.

Once the beans are tender, use the back of a spoon or a fork to partially mash about half of them directly in the pan — the mixture should be thick and chunky, not a smooth paste. Leave the rest of the beans whole for texture.
Add the minced garlic, cumin powder, lemon juice and a generous pinch of salt. Stir over low heat for 2 minutes so the garlic blooms gently in the warm beans.

Transfer to a shallow serving bowl or keep in the pan. Drizzle olive oil generously over the top. Scatter chopped coriander, parsley, fresh tomato and green chilli over the surface. Serve warm with flatbread for scooping — pita or any Indian roti works perfectly.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: lobia → Boiled chickpeas give a neutral, creamy result; soaked and boiled toor dal is an ultra-budget substitute; olive-oil → Any neutral oil; a few drops of sesame oil add a distinctive aroma; lemon → Half a lime or 1 tsp tamarind water for a rounder sourness; cumin → Half the amount of cumin seeds, dry-roasted and crushed, for a more intense earthy note
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Fava beans (broad beans) are not commonly stocked in Indian stores, but lobia (black-eyed peas / karamani) or chickpeas make excellent substitutes with only a slight difference in texture. Pressure-cook soaked lobia for 3 whistles instead of boiling. The dish is essentially a spiced mashed pulse — very close in spirit to a simple dal with olive oil and lemon.
